author: @elgorrion52
text: A great video and something I would like to try as a room heater. I would use caution in a confined space because there will still be products of combustion eg carbon dioxide which will need to be got rid of, and if there is too little air volume or ventilation, such as in a tent, then Carbon Monoxide could be produced. With that in mind, would this work with pure hydrogen as a fuel?

author: @travismoore7849
text: I had thought of using rare earth nitrate and a ceramic mantle but I know I am being silly as infrared heaters already use ceramics to get red hot on gas stoves. The incandescent material would only be to make light from the hot flames. Though that would be odd having a heater that was ceramic and incandescent that make both heat and lots of light. The idea was to mix clay with either a rare earth oxide or water soluble nitrate to make the ceramic element and have something to cut or poke fine holes all over the ceramic burner that would glow red hot and maybe be incandescent. I am sure there is a reason why this was never done.

author: @keithjurena9319
text:
Copper is a highly effective catalyst for ketone oxidation.  This also reduces copper oxides on the metallic surface.
This is utilized in brazing of copper as the flame acts as the flux, reducing oxide and allowing the filler metal to flow.

author: @McRootbeer
text: what about using sand as the wick?  it's cheap, available everywhere, temp resistant, has a high surface area, and will conform to the shape of any can in the supermarket 😁.  maybe throw the chromium oxide and sand in a jar, dissolve the other catalyst material in water, add to jar, seal and tumble for hours (could pad the outside of the jar and throw it in with the washing and drying for a few loads of laundry 😁) then pour into whatever can for the burner body and cook it in the oven.  might have to use more catalyst material, but if it works it would be safe, cheap, and accessable to anyone.  maybe use clay or plaster as a binder and make a paste to create higher surface area shapes.

replies.author: @SteveEh
replies.text: to test a cheap ebay Catalytic convertor; I lit a propane torch, held it to the honeycomb until it glowed red, then turned off the flame, and then turned back on the propane supply without relighting it. It continued to glow and give off heat as long as the propane continued. I would imagine you could devise a heater that worked off of this simple principle. good luck

author: @putteslaintxtbks5166
text: Though I never looked into, I've wondered about making a catalitic element to add to a wood stove so it wouldn't have ad much cleaner smoke and more heat out of the fuel, though one can buy them, they wouldn't be a custom fit. As a medium, I thought paper soaked in clay, woth hundreds of holes, triangles, or honeycomed, etc. in the size and shape wanted with a framework also of clayed paper. Do multi layers. Fire. Dip in catalist. Either have a metal frame or have bolt holes already in fired unit. Some catalists could prob be mixed in with the clay right off or as a glaze, if thin enough not to fill holes.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: It is an interesting idea, but it is probably easier to insulate it and get more air/oxygen flow into it.  A well designed wood heater/stove can combust efficiently enough to have little left over pollution.

replies.author: @putteslaintxtbks5166
replies.text: @justinw1765 Your right in a way. If you have alot of thermal mass and  catch alot of the heat, a fast, hot burn for an hour-hour and a half, then close air flow down just enough to let the coals burn down so you don't send the heat you've collected out the chimney. But when keeping a steady fire going, you need to keep air choked some, or the heat goes up the chimney and is wasted, but some fuel will be going up the chimney without being burned. A catalitic will do a second "burn" of alot of that. I heated a big 100 year plus old house for over a decade on only wood. Most of the winter, it was about 80°f on the main floor. If I gave it more air, it would have burned cleaner but been way to warm and gone through way more wood. The most efficent wood stoves today have catalitics built in them, that has been one of the biggest game changer in wood stoves the last few decades and way safer as chimney stays cleaner, so less risk of a chimney fire.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: IIRC Solid Oxide Fuel Cells use a similar principle and operate at temps around 200*C using either a zirconia catalyst or substrate. Acetone vapours do the same on hot copper. Ammonia does as well but can melt the copper and creates ammonium nitrate as a sideproduct.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Very good!   I see chromium oxide, cobalt oxide, and manganese dioxide available as pigments and glazes, that don't seem to require further oxidation. Is there any advantage to mixing and cooking the chemicals together vs buying the oxides outright as powders?

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: Good idea with one exception,  these are made as glazes ect.  You would have to cook off or otherwise remove the additives that make it safer to handle and in essence what it is meant for, ie.. a glaze.  Here is the caveat, even after cooking off those impurities, some residuals will remain and need further purification before use adding complications.  But keep thinking along those lines,  you have a great idea here if it is possible to purify those adulterated chemicals.  I love this train of thought!  Keep it up.

replies.author: @DrSpooglemon
replies.text: Solubility I'd imagine.

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text:
@jamesross1003 I found the raw powders on line and at Amazon.  I should have said "for" glazes rather than "as",  They seemed pretty cheap, and don't seem to be considered hazardous to ship in the US.  As pigments they should be soluable in water -- which may not matter much if applied to ceramic or glass wool as a substrate.  
I also found that while many newer OEM catalytic converters are expensive, an older universal replacement converter is cheaper than the smallest quantity of all the three powdered metal oxides together.

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @azlandpilotcar4450 Nice, let us know how it turns out!  Looking forward to your experiments.  Careful though as the chemicals can be dangerous.  Best of luck.

replies.author: @daroniussubdeviant3869
replies.text: fractional distillation like in an oil refinery. i've seen heavy tars gassified and fed through a pipe with aluminium powder to crack the long carbon chains down before being sent to the condenser.

replies.author: @mackelby1
replies.text: @daroniussubdeviant3869 starting  to sound like a very intense process

replies.author: @daroniussubdeviant3869
replies.text: @mackelby1 yep. pyrolysis is fascinating and there is a lot involved on a chemical level. if it is done a certain way everything can be reduced down to hydrogen and carbon monoxide which makes a brilliant fuel.

replies.author: @daroniussubdeviant3869
replies.text: @mackelby1 they way to do it is just to do it in two or more passes. collect the tar from the first pass and run it through again and again till it all breaks down.
